albums.title: Discovery
albums.reason: With 2940 all-time plays, Daft Punk is your most-loved artist ever — and Discovery is the record that defined a generation of dance music. Veridis Quo, Digital Love, and Superheroes all rank in your all-time top tracks, so this one deserves a deep revisit.

albums.title: OK Computer
albums.reason: Radiohead sits at 2050 all-time plays and Reckoner is one of your most-played tracks ever. OK Computer is the album that cemented their genius — Exit Music, No Surprises, and Paranoid Android feel built for late-night listening.

albums.title: Off the Wall
albums.reason: Michael Jackson sits at 1852 all-time plays and you've loved tracks like Billie Jean and Human Nature for years. Off the Wall is the slightly earlier, sun-drenched companion — Don't Stop 'Til You Get Enough and Rock With You are pure timeless joy.

albums.title: The Virgin Suicides
albums.reason: Air ranks at 1011 all-time plays and La femme d'argent is one of your favourite tracks. The Virgin Suicides is their most atmospheric and emotionally rich work — a perfect companion to Moon Safari that you may not have revisited in years.

albums.title: Hot Fuss
albums.reason: The Killers sit at 926 all-time plays and Mr. Brightside, A Dustland Fairytale, and All These Things That I've Done all appear in your top tracks. Hot Fuss is where it all started — a record that probably meant everything to you in the mid-2000s.

albums.title: Orbital (The Blue Album)
albums.reason: Orbital has 777 all-time plays and Don't Stop Me is deep in your top tracks. The Blue Album is their raw, euphoric debut — Chime alone is one of the great moments in electronic music, and this record predates the more polished sides you may know better.

albums.title: The North Borders
albums.reason: Bonobo appears in both your all-time and 12-month lists with 440 and 25 plays respectively, and Kerala and Kong are among your most-played tracks. The North Borders was a peak moment for him — Cirrus, Kiara, and First Fires sit in that rich mid-2010s downtempo sweet spot.

albums.title: Assume Form
albums.reason: James Blake is at 637 all-time plays and Postpone is your single most-played track. Assume Form is his most emotionally open record, blending the electronic minimalism you love with warmth and vulnerability — released in 2019, it sits perfectly in the recent vault era.

albums.title: There Is Love in You
albums.reason: Four Tet appears in your 12-month top artists with 32 plays, and Angel Echoes and Locked sit in your most-played tracks. There Is Love in You is his warmest, most meditative record — a perfect deep-listen for someone in your current headspace.

albums.title: The Last Resort
albums.reason: Trentemøller sits at 560 all-time plays and Moan, Take Me Into Your Skin, and Into the Trees are all in your played history. The Last Resort is his defining debut — a darker, more cinematic record that launched him, and one worth returning to after years away.

albums.title: The Devil's Walk
albums.reason: Apparat features heavily in your 12-month listening with 36 plays and A Hum Of Maybe is your most-played recent album. The Devil's Walk is his most song-based and emotionally heavy record — Goodbye and Not a Number already live in your history, so this album deserves a full revisit.

albums.title: II
albums.reason: Moderat appears across your play history with Rusty Nails and Bad Kingdom as firm favourites, and II is the record where everything clicked for them. It sits in a mid-2010s era that perfectly bridges your love of Apparat and electronic songwriting — a vault-era gem.

albums.title: Super Discount
albums.reason: Your 2940-play love of Daft Punk points directly to the French house scene they emerged from, and Étienne de Crécy was right there alongside them. Super Discount is a raw, propulsive French house classic that feels like a missing chapter from your collection.

albums.title: We Fell to Earth
albums.reason: Your deep love of Plaid, Boards of Canada, and Aphex Twin points to melodic IDM with emotional texture — Digitonal operated in exactly that space but remained almost entirely under the radar. We Fell to Earth is a gorgeous, overlooked gem from that era.

albums.title: Neveroddoreven
albums.reason: Given your love of The Avalanches, Gorillaz, and the more playful side of electronic music, I Monster's Neveroddoreven is a cult classic that fits perfectly — sample-heavy, melodic, and inventive in ways that should resonate strongly with your all-time taste.

albums.title: Chemistry Is What We Are
albums.reason: Simian Mobile Disco's Sleep Deprivation is one of your top tracks, and this is the earlier band those members came from — Chemistry Is What We Are is a weirder, more psychedelic indie-electronic record that should fascinate any fan of early-2000s experimentation.

albums.title: Avant Hard
albums.reason: Your love of Underworld, Leftfield, and the heavier end of British electronic music in the late 90s suggests Add N to (X) would hit hard — Avant Hard is a noisy, playful, analogue synth record that sits right in that era's adventurous spirit.

albums.title: Client
albums.reason: Your 771-play love of Justice and your affinity for cold, driving electronic music points toward Client — a British synth-pop duo who made sleek, minimal electronic pop in the early 2000s that feels like a direct peer to artists you love but probably slipped past you.

albums.title: Karma
albums.reason: Your 12-month arc is pulling you into jazz territory via Oscar Peterson, Bill Evans, and Portico Quartet — Ill Considered are a London quartet who make raw, improvised spiritual jazz with deep groove. Karma is the record where they fully arrived, and it sounds like it was made for your current headspace.

albums.title: Refuge
albums.reason: Your recent deep listening to Brambles, A Winged Victory for the Sullen, and Hania Rani suggests a pull toward sparse, emotionally honest music with voice and space — Caoilfhionn Rose's Refuge sits in exactly that territory, fragile and beautiful.

albums.title: Ki-Motion
albums.reason: Your 12-month listening to Dominik Eulberg, Portico Quartet, and the rhythmic-meditative end of things points toward this Japanese percussion ensemble from 1981 — Ki-Motion is an extraordinary minimalist record, rhythmically immersive and unlike anything else in your current rotation.

albums.title: The Shakedown
albums.reason: Your recent listening to St Germain, Nightmares on Wax, and the spiritual jazz crossover zone perfectly maps onto Tenderlonious — a London flautist and composer whose work blends jazz, Afrobeat, and club instincts in ways your current taste is clearly chasing.

albums.title: Day in the Life
albums.reason: Your 12-month pull toward Portico Quartet, Nils Frahm, and world-inflected jazz suggests Sarathy Korwar — a London-based Indian percussionist who fuses tabla rhythms with jazz and ambient in ways that feel deeply aligned with where your listening is heading.

albums.title: For Those of You Who Have Never (And Also Those Who Have)
albums.reason: Your 12-month listening to Brambles, Ben Lukas Boysen, and the hazier end of ambient points directly to Huerco S. — this record is a masterclass in blurry, tape-saturated ambient that dissolves time. It fits perfectly between the textural and the meditative in your current arc.

albums.title: Ardour
albums.reason: Given your love of Bonobo, Four Tet, and the melodic-beat crossover, Teebs' debut Ardour is a stunning blind spot to fill — it's warm, hazy, and full of micro-melodic detail that rewards the same kind of deep listening you're clearly giving your current favourites.

albums.title: Mordechai
albums.reason: You've played Mordechai tracks — Pelota, Time (You and I), If There Is No Question — repeatedly in the last few days. The full album deserves a proper sit-down listen this week; it's the richest and most textured thing they've made.

albums.title: Portico Quartet
albums.reason: Ruins has been on loop for you all week — multiple plays across multiple days — and this self-titled debut is where that whole sound was born. If you're in a Ruins mood, the rest of this album will feel like exactly the right place to be.

albums.title: Spells
albums.reason: Golden Times 1 has been a constant for you in the last 7 days across multiple plays. Spells is the album it lives on — introspective, architectural, and emotionally generous in ways that suit wherever you are this week.

albums.title: Evenfall
albums.reason: Sébastien Schuller has been in your top month artists with 10 plays and Heat Wave is in your recent-heard albums. Evenfall is his more ambient, evening-facing companion record — quieter and more expansive, perfect for the contemplative mode you're clearly in this week.

albums.title: Ambivalence Avenue
albums.reason: Bibio has turned up in your last-month listening and Lovers' Carvings sits in your history. Ambivalence Avenue is the record that put him on the map — a perfect blend of hazy lo-fi beats and warm acoustic guitar that fits your current floaty, introspective groove.

albums.title: Animal Magic
albums.reason: Bonobo has been a constant in your last month with 13 plays and you've explored Days to Come recently. Animal Magic is his raw, earliest record — dusty, organic beats and warmth that feels like a natural companion to the gentle, groove-forward music you're living in right now.

songs.title: Nightvision
songs.reason: Nightvision has 96 plays in your all-time top tracks — a strange, barely-there moment from Discovery that you clearly returned to obsessively. It's the kind of track that only reveals itself after years of listening.

songs.title: Sparks
songs.reason: Röyksopp sits at 1062 all-time plays and The Girl and the Robot and Remind Me are in your top tracks. Sparks is from Melody A.M. — their most emotionally raw moment, built for late nights and deep nostalgia exactly like this tier calls for.

songs.title: Music: Response
songs.reason: The Chemical Brothers sit at 556 all-time plays and Block Rockin' Beats and The Sunshine Underground are in your history. Music: Response is one of their greatest moments — a relentless, euphoric track from Surrender that belongs deep in the vault.

songs.title: Phat Planet
songs.reason: Leftfield has 1016 all-time plays and Release the Pressure is one of your most-played tracks ever. Phat Planet — from Rhythm and Stealth — is their most hypnotic and physically overwhelming achievement, and it probably hit you hard the first time you heard it.

songs.title: Comfortably Numb
songs.reason: Pink Floyd sits at 1199 all-time plays and Comfortably Numb is already in your broader play history as a standout. From The Wall, it's one of the great guitar solos in rock history — a track your ears know deeply but the body still responds to every time.

songs.title: Teardrop
songs.reason: Massive Attack sits at 534 all-time plays and Teardrop is already a firm favourite in your history. From Mezzanine, it's one of the most emotionally precise records of the 90s — this track in particular feels like pure deep-vault nostalgia.

songs.title: Cirrus
songs.reason: Bonobo has 440 all-time plays and Cirrus is one of his most beloved tracks from The North Borders. It's already deep in your history — a perfect recent-vault moment that captures the mid-2010s electronic scene you were clearly immersed in.

songs.title: Angel Echoes
songs.reason: Four Tet has 32 plays in your 12-month list and Angel Echoes sits in your play history from There Is Love in You. It's one of his most luminous tracks — a recent-vault gem that bridges your love of electronic music with the more meditative direction you're heading now.

songs.title: Rykketid
songs.reason: Trentemøller has 560 all-time plays and Rykketid already sits in your play history from Fixion. It's a restless, hypnotic track that represents his more recent, darker phase — and sits perfectly in the recent vault era between nostalgia and now.

songs.title: Nervous Tics
songs.reason: Maribou State has 434 all-time plays and Nervous Tics and Beginner's Luck both sit deep in your play history. This track from Kingdoms in Colour is one of their most emotionally charged moments — a recent vault peak that probably hit hard when it came out.

songs.title: Near Light
songs.reason: Ólafur Arnalds has 25 plays in your 12-month list and Near Light sits in your play history from re:member. It's one of his most beautiful moments — a delicate, aching track that represents the modern classical direction you've been moving toward in recent years.

songs.title: Reminder
songs.reason: Moderat's Rusty Nails and Reminder both sit in your play history, and with 66 plays, Rusty Nails is one of your most-played tracks. Reminder from III is the emotional counterpart — a slower, more haunting piece that captures the ache at the heart of their best work.

songs.title: Prix Choc
songs.reason: Your 2940-play love of Daft Punk traces directly back to the French house scene, and Prix Choc is the track that established de Crécy as one of its most inventive figures — a driving, mechanical groove that feels like a missing piece of your all-time electronic story.

songs.title: Ultraviolet
songs.reason: Your love of Aphex Twin, Boards of Canada, and the deeper end of electronic music points toward Deepchord — an alias steeped in dub techno and ambient that operates at the same slow, meditative frequency your ears clearly love. Ultraviolet is a perfect entry point.

songs.title: Toomorrow
songs.reason: Your love of The Avalanches, Moby, and sample-based electronic music in the all-time charts points directly to Luke Vibert's Wagon Christ alias — Toomorrow is a joyful, densely layered beat record that has the same warmth and wit as your favourite records from that era.

songs.title: You Stand Uncertain
songs.reason: Given your love of James Blake, Burial, and the more emotional end of British electronic music, Falty DL is a natural discovery — You Stand Uncertain is a beautiful, slightly broken record full of grief and warmth that sits right alongside those all-time favourites.

songs.title: Tides
songs.reason: Your deep love of Plaid, Aphex Twin, and melodic IDM in the all-time charts maps perfectly onto Arovane — a German producer whose Tides record from 2000 is a glittering, emotionally warm IDM classic that exists in exactly the space you love but likely slipped past you.

songs.title: Fragments
songs.reason: Your all-time love of Massive Attack, Portishead, and emotionally heavy electronic music with vocals points straight to Submotion Orchestra — Fragments is a gorgeous Leeds collective record that blends jazz, trip-hop, and soul in ways your all-time taste clearly craves.

songs.title: Qadar
songs.reason: Your 12-month devotion to Bill Withers, Charles Bradley, and Erykah Badu points toward Nick Hakim — a Brooklyn artist making deeply personal, psychedelic soul music. Qadar is a slow-burning, intimate track that sounds like it was written for late-night listening exactly like yours.

songs.title: Kintsukuroi
songs.reason: Your recent listening to St Germain, Nils Frahm, and the jazz-adjacent emotional space you're in right now points toward Alabaster DePlume's earlier, rawer work — Kintsukuroi is a fragile, searching piece that predates the albums you may already know.

songs.title: Hannah
songs.reason: Your recent listening to Brambles, Hania Rani, and the quietly emotional end of your current arc suggests Lomelda — a Texas songwriter whose Hannah is one of the most tender and spacious records of recent years, sitting beautifully alongside your current headspace.

songs.title: The Window
songs.reason: Your 12-month listening to Oscar Peterson, Bill Evans, and the jazz direction you're clearly exploring points toward Cécile McLorin Salvant — one of the finest jazz vocalists working today. The Window is an intimate duo record that rewards exactly the kind of deep listening you give to Bill Withers.

songs.title: Drift
songs.reason: Your recent listening to Apparat, Ben Lukas Boysen, and the beat-ambient crossover points toward Nosaj Thing — a Los Angeles producer whose Drift is a beautifully sparse, emotionally affecting beat record that sits right alongside the introspective electronic music dominating your 12-month arc.

songs.title: Treny
songs.reason: Your 12-month listening to Brambles, A Winged Victory for the Sullen, and the more austere ambient end of your taste points toward Jacaszek — a Polish composer whose Treny is built from 16th-century lute music processed into something hauntingly beautiful and modern.

songs.title: Father Bird, Mother Bird
songs.reason: You played this from Mordechai just days ago and it's clearly resonating — a weightless, hypnotic track that captures everything Khruangbin do best. This week it belongs back in your ears at full volume.

songs.title: Lullaby for Humans
songs.reason: With Ruins on constant loop this week, Lullaby for Humans from Memory Streams is the natural companion — slower and even more inward-looking, it captures the same meditative quality you're clearly seeking right now.

songs.title: Golden Times 2
songs.reason: Golden Times 1 has been in your most-played tracks this week — Golden Times 2 is the quiet continuation, just as radiant and worth giving the same attention you've been giving its companion.

songs.title: Kong
songs.reason: Bonobo has had 13 plays in your last month and Kong sits deep in your play history — a restless, propulsive track from Migration that has the same warm-electronic energy defining your current week.

songs.title: Lovers' Carvings
songs.reason: Bibio appeared in your last-month listening and Lovers' Carvings is already in your history — a warm, summery loop of guitar and beats from Ambivalence Avenue that fits the floaty, unhurried mood you're clearly in this week.

songs.title: Ambling Alp
songs.reason: Yeasayer appeared in your last 7 days and O.N.E. from Odd Blood is in your most recent tracks. Ambling Alp is the album's other great moment — a soaring, propulsive anthem that captures the more energetic end of your current mood.

djSets.setName: Daphni - FOLD, London. Nov 22, 2025. 7hr DJ set
Fred again b2b Thomas Bangalter of Daft Punk FULL 4K Set - USB002 at Alexandra Palace in London UK
